domain_applicability.reason: Leistungsbewertung, Multipath-Minderung und simulationsgestützte Evaluierung sind als Methoden übertragbar.

hypotheses.statement: Gegenüber klassischen streckenseitigen Ortungstechnologien im Bahnumfeld bietet GNSS den Hauptvorteil, dass die Zugortung mit deutlich geringerer Abhängigkeit von track-side Infrastruktur erfolgen kann, was Flexibilität, Wartbarkeit und Lebenszykluskosten verbessert.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die bahnspezifischen Dokumente beschreiben, dass train-centric Systeme Gleisfreimeldung und Zugortung ohne Balisen/Track Circuits realisieren sollen und dadurch reduzierte Beteiligung streckenseitiger Ausrüstung, bessere Maintainability und höhere whole-life-cycle cost efficiency erreichen.

hypotheses.statement: Gegenüber fahrzeugbasierten Einzelsensoren wie Odometrie, Doppler-Radar oder INS liegt der zentrale Vorteil von GNSS in der kontinuierlichen absoluten globalen Positions- und Zeitreferenz; robuste Bahnanwendungen erfordern jedoch typischerweise Sensorfusion, weil GNSS allein in Tunneln, Abschattung und Störumgebungen ausfällt oder degradiert.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Dokumente heben GNSS als Quelle präziser globaler Position, Geschwindigkeit und Zeit hervor, während IMU/INS Drift akkumulieren und Odometrie/Doppler nur relative Bewegung liefern; zugleich wird explizit beschrieben, dass GNSS in Tunneln und schwierigen Umgebungen Lücken hat und daher mit INS/Odometrie fusioniert werden muss.

hypotheses.statement: Ein regulatorisch-interoperabilitätsbezogener Vorteil von GNSS gegenüber alternativen Bahn-Ortungstechnologien ist plausibel, weil GNSS-Leistung über standardisierte, systemübergreifende Kennzahlen wie Genauigkeit, Verfügbarkeit, Kontinuität/Integrität und PDOP beschrieben sowie in Simulations- und Zertifizierungsverfahren bewertet werden kann; die Dokumente belegen dies jedoch stärker für GNSS-Standards als für einen expliziten Überlegenheitsvergleich zu Konkurrenztechnologien.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Quellen nennen BDS/GPS-Performance-Standards, internationale/regionalen Zertifizierungsbezug sowie RAMS- und Integritätsmetriken für GNSS-basierte Bahnanwendungen. Damit ist Standardisierbarkeit gut belegt; ein direkter Nachweis, dass dies regulatorisch überlegen gegenüber anderen Bahnortungstechnologien ist, bleibt jedoch begrenzt.

hypotheses.statement: Der betriebliche Mehrwert von GNSS ist besonders in digitalen und train-centric Anwendungen hoch, weil GNSS Virtual Balises, autonome Zugzustandserfassung, Moving-Block-nahe Konzepte und digitale Test-/Simulationsverfahren unterstützt; dieser Mehrwert ist aber an Absicherungen wie Integritätsüberwachung, Mehrsensorfusion und Umgebungsmodellierung gebunden.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Bahndokumente verknüpfen GNSS mit Virtual Balise, train integrity monitoring, train-centric signaling und digital-railway Testplattformen. Gleichzeitig wird klar, dass Safety-Anwendungen GNSS stand-alone nicht ausreichen lassen und daher Monitoring/Fusion nötig ist; der operative Nutzen ist also hoch, aber nicht uneingeschränkt.

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,14,15]. In contrast to studies that mainly investigate GNSS signal inter- ferences in isolation, this paper integrates environmental factors and signal interferences into the formal model of the positioning system. By utilizing model simulation, the proposed approach enables a quantita- tive evaluation of the system’s performance. This method provides a systematic analysis and verification of the interactions among signal interferences, environmental conditions, and train positioning. Due to the rigorous mathematical foundation, formal methods have gained recognition as effective tools for system modeling and verification. In this work, we adopt CPNs [16] as the formal modeling framework to study the impact of signal interferences and environ- mental factors on GNSS-based train positioning. Petri nets are formal modeling languages that are well-known for systems characterized as concurrent, synchronous, distributed, etc. Some application examples adopting petri nets as the means of description for railway signaling systems can be found in Refs. [17–22]. 3. System modeling with CPNs Fig. 1 illustrates the modeling framework of a GNSS-based train positioning system comprising the following main parts: GNSS Re- ceiver, Environment Scenarios, Interference Signals, GNSS Position Solution, and Evaluation Module. The module GNSS Receiver selects specific environment scenarios and receives GNSS signals. The module Environment Scenarios is composed of three submodules: Open Area Scenario, Mountain Occlusion Scenario, and Tunnel and Exit Tunnel Scenario. These submodules simulate various real-world conditions while generating GNSS signal interferences, i.e., GNSS signals are interfered with by AM, FM, and pulse signals. In the module GNSS Positioning Solution, the EKF algorithm is employed to process interfered GNSS signals for estimating the train’s position. The module Evaluation compares the calculated position with the reference position and outputs the positioning error. Fig. 2 clearly illustrates the hierarchical relationships between modules of the CPN model, providing a structured view of their layered organization. The declarations of the model are categorized and summarized in two tables for clarity. Table 1 provides the colset declarations and their respective descriptions, while Table 2 provides the variable declara- tions and their respective descriptions. 3.1. Top-level of the positioning system model The top-level of the CPN model is

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets Shuting Chena,b, Daohua Wua,b,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b National Research Center of Railway Safety Assessment, Collaborative Innovation Center of Railway Traffic Safety, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Jiang Liua,c,d, Siqi Wanga,d ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS-based train positioning Positioning performance Environment scenario GNSS signal interference Colored petri Nets Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-based continuous and accurate train positioning is one of the key technologies for advanced train operations such as train virtual coupling. However, GNSS-based train posi- tioning faces significant challenges in real-world scenarios due to environmental complexities and signal in- terferences. Considering this issue, this paper presents an approach for modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning systems using Colored Petri Nets (CPNs). By systematically modeling the GNSS signal reception and processing process, the performance of the positioning system under various environment scenarios is evaluated. The system model integrates three types of interference signals (i.e., Amplitude Modulation (AM) signals, Frequency Modulation (FM) signals, and pulse signals) while incorporating environ- mental factors such as terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding. Additionally, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) algorithm is employed to process GNSS observation data, providing accurate train position estimations. The simulation results demonstrate that signal interferences and complex environmental conditions significantly affect the GNSS-based positioning accuracy. This study offers a comprehensive framework for evaluating the performance of GNSS-based train positioning systems in different scenarios, highlighting critical factors that influence positioning accuracy and stability. 1. Introduction With the increasing demand for operation safety and efficiency in rail transportation, train virtual coupling has been proposed and be- come a hot research topic in recent years. For virtual coupling

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)/Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) are key components in autonomous driving systems, helping to determine the position, speed, and orientation of the vehicle. GNSS is a satellite-based navigation system that provides precise positioning and timing information anywhere on Earth. By triangulat- ing signals from multiple satellites, a GNSS receiver can calculate the vehicle’s position (latitude, longitude, and altitude) and speed. GNSS is widely used for global positioning and mapping, especially in open areas such as highways. In autonomous driving, it is one of the most commonly used vehicle positioning sensors. GNSS provides precise global positioning, which enables accurate positioning in open environments such as highways or rural roads. Differential GPS (DGPS), Real-Time Kinematic GPS (RTK-GPS), and Precise Point Positioning (PPP) technologies can be used to improve the accuracy of GNSS. The differential global positioning system (DGPS) operation consists of a reference station and a rover. Both stations use GPS receivers to receive positioning data from GPS satellites, use the positioning data collected by the reference station to calculate the positioning error, and transmit the error correction to the rover to improve the positioning accuracy [27]. RTK can achieve real-time centimeter-level positioning accuracy through double-difference ambigu- ity resolution (AR) [28]. Compared with differential techniques, precise point positioning (PPP) has a significant advantage in that it can accurately determine the position of a GNSS rover receiver by using external corrections from the Internet or dedicated correction satellites [29]. While both DGPS and RTK can achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. content.abstract_or_snippet: achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The IMU can provide information about the vehicle’s attitude, velocity, and direction. The IMU helps determine vehicle motion between GNSS updates and ensures continuous tracking of the vehicle’s path, especially in areas where GNSS signals may be temporarily lost, such as tunnels and dense urban areas. The IMU provides real-time feedback on the ve- hicle’s motion, which is critical for continued tracking, especially in dynamic environments. As the positioning error accumulates during the vehicle’s driving, the IMU signal error accumulation will drift over time, so the IMU needs to continuously correct the estimated position. The data fusion of GNSS and IMU can achieve vehicle state estimation and ensure a continuous positioning process. GNSS and IMU data are often fused with other sensors such as LiDAR and radar to improve positioning accuracy and build a complete picture of the vehicle’s surroundings. By fusing these data sources, autonomous vehicles are able to navigate in a variety of environments, even without direct GNSS signals. There are currently several models for combining GNSS and IMU data. (1) GNSS/IMU integration: combining GNSS data with IMU information can create a dead reckoning system that continuously estimates the vehicle’s position when GNSS signals are unavailable. In this model, the IMU uses accelerometers and gyroscopes to track vehicle motion and orientation to fill in the gaps in GNSS data. (2) Tightly coupled system: in this, GNSS and IMU data are fused and processed in real time. This can improve the accuracy of vehicle positioning, es- pecially in challenging environments where GNSS signals are weak or unstable. (3) Loosely coupled system: in this system, GNSS and IMU operate independently and merge the data in the post-processing stage. Compared with the tightly coupled system, this method is less computationally intensive, but less accurate. World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 8 of 34 To mitigate the limitations of GNSS and IMU, these sensors are often fused with other perception technologies, such as LiDAR, radar, and cameras. GNSS/IMU systems are often integrated with LiDAR and radar sensors to enhance the vehicle’s perception of its surroundings. Generally, in autonomous driving systems, it is a common practice to increase the overall positioning and mapping performance by fusing two or more sensors. Reference [152] proposed a camera–radar sensor fusion framework based on vehicle component (rear corner) detection and positioning to improve the stability of vehicle positioning. The main idea of this method is to enhance the azimuth accuracy of radar information by detecting and locating the rear corner of the target vehicle from the image. The proposed method can effectively track the corner points of the vehicle using only one visible corner even in occluded scenes. GPS may experience signal attenuation in indoor spaces and urban canyons, and IMU may drift over time due to error accumulation when integrating acceleration to determine velocity and position. Reference [153] uses an unscented Kalman filter (UKF) Bayesian filter to fuse GPS and IMU data. Reference [154] uses the error state Kalman filter (ESKF) and the piecewise Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS) smoothing algorithm to fuse GNSS and IMU to improve the positioning accuracy and robustness of GNSS and IMU sensors. The proposed ESKF-RTS method provides more accurate and reliable positioning for autonomous vehicles, especially in environments where GNSS signals are unreliable. Reference [155] proposes a fusion algorithm that combines the Kalman filter (KF) with a new INS/GPS neural network framework (GI-NN) to assist INS in reducing the accumulated navigation error during GPS signal loss. Reference [156] studies the information fusion of GPS and INS sensors using the Kalman filter. GNSS testing standard and regulations As illustrated in the introduction part, the public service performance evaluation of GNSS covers two key aspects: SIS performance and system service performance. As the BeiDou Navigation Satellite System Open Service Performance Standard (BDS-OS-PS-3.0) defines, the BDS service performance should cover 5 core indicators: positioning accuracy, velocity measurement accuracy, timing accuracy, PDOP availability, and positioning service availability, as shown in Fig. 4. In terms of positioning accuracy, the specification clarifies the statistical standard of geographical location mea- surement error and specifies the allowable error range under different geographical areas and environmental conditions. The evaluation of speed measurement accuracy is concerned with the accuracy of the user equip- ment to measure the motion speed, which is used in dynamic scenarios, such as transportation and railway applications. Timing accuracy is aimed at high-precision time synchronization services, which are critical cap- abilities in fields such as communications, energy, and scientific experi- ments. The availability of PDOP is an important index to evaluate the effect of geometric configuration on positioning accuracy and to measure the degree of support of satellite layout to the stability of positioning solution. Location service availability measures the ability to provide continuous and reliable service at a given time and area from the perspective of the overall functionality of the system. In addition, the standard also specifies the measurement methods of this performance, including the evaluation environment, data collection method, calculation method, and evaluation cycle specific requirements. content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train- centric railway signaling Baigen Caia,b,⁎ a School of Computer and Information Technology, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c School of Geodesy and Geomatics, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China d GNSS Research Center, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China e Space Engineering University, Beijing 101416, China , Jingnan Liuc,d, Xurong Donge, Jiang Liub ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: Railway signaling Train control Global Navigation Satellite System Sensor data fusion Virtual Balise Train integrity Performance evaluation The application of Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) in the intelligent railway systems is rapidly developing all over the world. With the GNSS-based train positioning and moving state perception, the au- tonomy and flexibility of a novel train control system can be greatly enhanced over the existing solutions relying on the track-side facilities. Considering the safety critical features of the railway signaling applications, the GNSS stand-alone mode may not be sufficient to satisfy the practical requirements. In this paper, the key technologies for applying GNSS in novel train-centric railway signaling systems are investigated, including the multi-sensor data fusion, Virtual Balise (VB) capturing and messaging, train integrity monitoring and system performance evaluation. According to the practical characteristics of the novel train control system under the moving block mode, the details of the key technologies are introduced. Field demonstration results of a novel train control system using the presented technologies under the practical railway operation conditions are presented to il- lustrate the achievable performance feature of autonomous train state perception using BeiDou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) and related solutions. It reveals the great potentials of these key technologies in the next generation train control system and other GNSS-based railway implementations. 1. High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 and the Doppler radar, also show great potentials in realizing the au- tonomous train positioning with a low cost over the Balise-based solu- tions [4]. However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. Virtual Balise The concept of VB (Virtual Balise) can effectively integrate the sa- tellite positioning technology into existing train control system frame- works without modification to the specifications [7,8]. VB capture is acknowledged by matching the train position and the preset VB re- ference position along the track [9]. Within a specific distance limit, a success matching will enable the determination of the time instant when a train passes through the target VB. Then, the VB telegram can be ex- tracted and transmitted to the ATP (Automatic Train Protection) system kernel. Based on the existing VB capture mechanism, a pre-capture de- cision logic is necessary to effectively reduce the risk of missed VB capturing. A VB capture recognition method based on the forward search strategy is proposed to accurately determine the position and time of VB capture, which improves the VB capture performance over conventional solution requires a pre-defined capture interval. 3.1. Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways. 1. Introduction Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) have seen vast applications in the transportation domain. The integration of Global Positioning System (GPS) and Beidou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) in railway is efficient for modernizing the railway network, GNSS is increasingly becoming a fundamental pillar not only for non-safety applications, but also for im- proved maintenance, automated train operation, and safety- related appli- cations, including the Next Generation of Train Control (NGTC) and re- novation of the train on-board equipment, all these will increase the railway system capacity, efficiency and safety of all railways [1]. To apply GNSS in railway train operation, the GNSS performance testing and verification is the essential procedure for the system to be adopted and further listed as mandatory in the system design and development. The certification of the GNSS-based train localization systems is also necessary to provide concrete validation or testing application services to carry out simulation tests with evaluation of the platform and environmental condi- tion simulations [2]. Beidou has a set of technical and regulatory standards, including the BDS Performance Standard, BDS Signal Interface Control Document (ICD), and technical requirements for user equipment. The BDS Signal In Space (SIS) and service performance characteristics include ac- curacy, availability, compatibility, and interoperability [3]. GPS has also published standard positioning service performance standards to guide the user for civil uses worldwide, the performance metrics are GPS SIS accuracy, continuity, and availability [4]. So both BDS and GPS are focusing on ac- curacy as the foundation for the GNSS performance [5]. The performance needs to be assessed and as the performances are all credited to the equipment, the equipment itself needs to be certified. The certification procedure, it is governed by various international and regional standards, such as the accuracy standards by BDS and GPS, the test and simulation standard procedures, the simulation must meet possible (as sensible to the GNSS) to make the test results plausible. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 116–124 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article , Wenzheng Qiaoa, Baigen Caia,b,c, Jian Wanga,b,c, Jiang Liua,b,c, Yuchen Zhaoa, Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways Debiao Lua,b,c,⁎ Chunjie Qiaod, Dirk Spiegele a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Beijing Engineering Center for EMC and GNSS Technologies, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Hunan Matrix Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., Changsha 410205, China e DB InfraGO AG, Berlin 10557, Germany ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS testing Railway train localization 3D environment modelling Digital track map Zero on-site testing The integration of Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) technology into railway train control systems is a crucial step toward achieving the vision of a digital railway. Traditional train control systems undergo extensive in-house tests and prolonged field tests for certification and approval before operational deployment, leading to high costs, delays, and operational disruptions. This paper introduces a GNSS-based train control localization framework which eliminates the need for on-site testing by leveraging train movement dynamics and 3D en- vironment modeling to create a zero on-site testing platform. The proposed framework simulates train move- ment and the surrounding 3D environment using collected railway line location data and environmental attri- butes to generate realistic multipath signals and obscuration effects. This approach enables comprehensive laboratory-based case studies for train localization, reducing the huge amount test of needed for physical field trials. The framework is established in house, using the data collected at the Test Base of China Academy of Railway Sciences (Circular Railway). Results from the open area and cutting environment tests demonstrate high localization accuracy repeatability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways.

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Early in the 1990s, the United States introduces the concept of the Positive Train control System (PTS) [1], which relies on Global Posi- tioning System (GPS), accelerometers, and fiber-optic gyroscopes for the train positioning function. By December 2020, the United States achieved 100 % implementation of Positive Train Control (PTC) across all required railroads, marking a historic milestone in rail safety and interoperability [2]. CLUG 2.0 project [3] launched in 2023 in Europe aims to enhance the European Rail Traffic Management System (ERTMS) by integrating an on-board GNSS+EGNOS-based multi-sensor fusion architecture. In China, the China State Railway Group Co., Ltd. introduced a new train control system, the Chinese Train Control System-New (CTCS-N) [4], which proposed a “train-borne centric” train operation control method. This system incorporates on-board GNSS positioning, speed sensors, and electronic railway maps to achieve train localization. GNSS has been acknowledged as a pivotal enabler in im- proving the cost efficiency and automation level of the train control system. In complex railway operation scenarios, such as urban canyons, tunnels, and stations with cover, the limited GNSS signal visibility, and weak observation geometry would greatly decrease the positioning performance. Thus, the integration solutions are typically proposed for the train safety positioning. A typical integration solution for GNSS- based train positioning is based on the data fusion with both GNSS and Inertial Navigation System (INS) sensors. The GNSS/INS integrated solution is capable of providing full navigation solutions of position, velocity, and attitude in high positioning accuracy, high updating rate even under the GNSS limited scenario when the visible satellites are less than four. Additionally, there is an increasing concern that GNSS is also vulnerable to radio frequency jamming, due to the weak signal power of the GNSS signal. More and more GNSS disturbance events are reported because of jamming, in worldwide, including maritime applications [5,6], aviation applications [7], and also railway applications [8,9]. GNSS in-band interferences, for example, intentional jamming shows a source of potential threats to the safe, secure, and resilient operation of a dense railway system utilizing GNSS as the main positioning method

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning Zhuojian Caoa, Jiang Liua,b,c,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Wei Jianga,b,c, Baigen Caia,b,c ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS jamming Deeply-coupled integration GNSS/INS Railway safety and efficiency increasingly rely on precise train positioning. The integration of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in- frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi- tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio Frequency (RF) jamming threats. 1. Introduction Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in- volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec- tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Liso Nicolás [13] used ray tracing to describe the channel characteristics between the satellite and the ground receiver and ex- tracted information from it to design a more positive algorithm, redu- cing the influence of multipath effects on positioning. This paper introduces a zero on-site testing scheme for GNSS perfor- mance evaluation for railway train control system adoption. The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the problems and challenges in the methods for zero on-site testing, Section 3 introduces the key technol- ogies in the testing procedure. After that, Section 4 introduces the data collected using the zero on-site testing platform, and Section 5 delivers the conclusion of the testing performance and characteristics. 2) GNSS signal propagation: with the train movement, the GNSS signal propagation in the real world can be affected by the environment along the railway track. As the optimized signal to be propagated is the Line-Of-Sight (LOS) signal, due to the obstacles along the track, the GNSS signal might be reflected, refracted, and blocked. Thus, environmental modelling is the basis for simulation. There are still many challenges in addressing scientific problems. The main problems to be discussed in this paper is the environmental modelling forming the ray-tracing procedure, and the train movement that integrates the environment and train location to generate the ap- propriate GNSS signal for the zero on-site testing. 2. Problems description 3. Key technologies in zero on-site testing of GNSS Since the benefits of zero on-site testing have been illustrated, the state-of-the-art for simulation of GNSS has also been demonstrated, but there are still scientific problems and challenges to be addressed for implementing zero on-site testing. The zero on-site testing procedure shall be composed of the procedure starting with the test control and management, then the train movement and simulation. With the si- mulated train trajectory similar to Fig. 1, the GNSS signal simulation and the modeled 3D environment together can generate the GNSS signal by the GNSS simulator, the simulated GNSS signal propagation needs to be modeled in the simulator to evaluate the Non-Line-Of-Sight (NLOS) multipath effects estimations [14]. updated_hypotheses.statement: Gegenüber klassischen streckenseitigen Ortungstechnologien im Bahnumfeld bietet GNSS den Hauptvorteil, dass die Zugortung mit deutlich geringerer Abhängigkeit von track-side Infrastruktur erfolgen kann, was Flexibilität, Wartbarkeit und Lebenszykluskosten verbessert.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese passt sehr gut zur Bahnevidenz: Mehrere bahnspezifische Quellen beschreiben GNSS-basierte, train-centric Ortung als Mittel zur Reduktion streckenseitiger Infrastruktur und nennen explizit erhöhte Flexibilität, verbesserte Maintainability und bessere Whole-Life-Cycle-Kosteneffizienz. Ein Falsifikationsversuch anhand von Randbedingungen zeigt zwar, dass GNSS für Safety-Anwendungen nicht stand-alone genügt, widerspricht aber nicht dem behaupteten Hauptvorteil gegenüber klassischer track-side Ortung.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Gegenüber fahrzeugbasierten Einzelsensoren wie Odometrie, Doppler-Radar oder INS liegt der zentrale Vorteil von GNSS in der kontinuierlichen absoluten globalen Positions- und Zeitreferenz; robuste Bahnanwendungen erfordern jedoch typischerweise Sensorfusion, weil GNSS allein in Tunneln, Abschattung und Störumgebungen ausfällt oder degradiert.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Evidenz stützt die Hypothese direkt und spezifisch: GNSS liefert absolute globale Positions- und Zeitinformation, während IMU/INS Drift akkumulieren und relative Sensoren Ausfälle von GNSS nur überbrücken. Mehrere Dokumente nennen zugleich die Grenzen von GNSS in Tunneln, Abschattung und Störumgebungen und begründen daher Sensorfusion als Standardansatz. Der Falsifikationsversuch findet keine Gegenbelege, sondern bestätigt die Boundary Condition der Hypothese.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Ein regulatorisch-interoperabilitätsbezogener Vorteil von GNSS gegenüber alternativen Bahn-Ortungstechnologien ist plausibel, weil GNSS-Leistung über standardisierte, systemübergreifende Kennzahlen wie Genauigkeit, Verfügbarkeit, Kontinuität/Integrität und PDOP beschrieben sowie in Simulations- und Zertifizierungsverfahren bewertet werden kann; die Dokumente belegen dies jedoch stärker für GNSS-Standards als für einen expliziten Überlegenheitsvergleich zu Konkurrenztechnologien.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese ist teilweise gut gestützt: Die Dokumente belegen klar, dass GNSS über standardisierte Leistungskennzahlen wie Genauigkeit, Verfügbarkeit, Timing/Integrität und PDOP beschrieben sowie in Simulations-, Test- und Zertifizierungsverfahren bewertet wird. Weniger direkt belegt ist jedoch die behauptete regulatorische bzw. interoperabilitätsbezogene Überlegenheit gegenüber alternativen Bahn-Ortungstechnologien. Der Falsifikationsversuch ergibt daher keine Widerlegung, aber eine Evidenzlücke hinsichtlich des expliziten Vergleichs mit angrenzenden Technologien.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Der betriebliche Mehrwert von GNSS ist besonders in digitalen und train-centric Anwendungen hoch, weil GNSS Virtual Balises, autonome Zugzustandserfassung, Moving-Block-nahe Konzepte und digitale Test-/Simulationsverfahren unterstützt; dieser Mehrwert ist aber an Absicherungen wie Integritätsüberwachung, Mehrsensorfusion und Umgebungsmodellierung gebunden.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese zeigt gute semantische Passung und ausreichende Tiefe: Bahndokumente verbinden GNSS explizit mit train-centric Signaling, Virtual Balises, Integritätsüberwachung, Performance-Evaluation und digitalen Test-/Simulationsverfahren. Gleichzeitig wird mehrfach betont, dass GNSS stand-alone für sicherheitskritische Anwendungen nicht ausreicht und Absicherungen wie Integritätslogik, Mehrsensorfusion und Umgebungsmodellierung erforderlich sind. Der Falsifikationsversuch bestätigt daher eher die eingegrenzten Bedingungen der Hypothese als sie zu widerlegen.

challenge_findings.description: Bahnspezifische Evidenz bestätigt, dass GNSS-basierte train-centric Ansätze die Abhängigkeit von streckenseitiger Infrastruktur reduzieren und dadurch Wartbarkeit sowie Lebenszykluskosten verbessern.

Die Dokumente stützen den Vorteil von GNSS als Quelle absoluter globaler Positions- und Zeitreferenz und zeigen gleichzeitig, dass INS/IMU und weitere Sensoren erforderlich sind, um GNSS-Ausfälle in Tunneln, urbanen Abschattungen und Störumgebungen zu überbrücken.

Die Quellen belegen standardisierte GNSS-Leistungsmetriken und Zertifizierungs-/Simulationsbezug, liefern aber nur begrenzte direkte Vergleichsevidenz dafür, dass GNSS regulatorisch oder interoperabilitätsbezogen alternativen Bahn-Ortungstechnologien überlegen ist.

GNSS unterstützt Virtual Balises, train-centric Anwendungen und digitale Test-/Simulationsverfahren; die Evidenz begrenzt diesen Mehrwert jedoch klar auf Architekturen mit Integritätsüberwachung, Sensorfusion und Umgebungsmodellierung.
Für sicherheitskritische Bahnanwendungen ist GNSS allein nicht ausreichend; Signalabschattung, Interferenz und Integritätsanforderungen begrenzen den Stand-alone-Einsatz.

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,14,15]. In contrast to studies that mainly investigate GNSS signal inter- ferences in isolation, this paper integrates environmental factors and signal interferences into the formal model of the positioning system. By utilizing model simulation, the proposed approach enables a quantita- tive evaluation of the system’s performance. This method provides a systematic analysis and verification of the interactions among signal interferences, environmental conditions, and train positioning. Due to the rigorous mathematical foundation, formal methods have gained recognition as effective tools for system modeling and verification. In this work, we adopt CPNs [16] as the formal modeling framework to study the impact of signal interferences and environ- mental factors on GNSS-based train positioning. Petri nets are formal modeling languages that are well-known for systems characterized as concurrent, synchronous, distributed, etc. Some application examples adopting petri nets as the means of description for railway signaling systems can be found in Refs. [17–22]. 3. System modeling with CPNs Fig. 1 illustrates the modeling framework of a GNSS-based train positioning system comprising the following main parts: GNSS Re- ceiver, Environment Scenarios, Interference Signals, GNSS Position Solution, and Evaluation Module. The module GNSS Receiver selects specific environment scenarios and receives GNSS signals. The module Environment Scenarios is composed of three submodules: Open Area Scenario, Mountain Occlusion Scenario, and Tunnel and Exit Tunnel Scenario. These submodules simulate various real-world conditions while generating GNSS signal interferences, i.e., GNSS signals are interfered with by AM, FM, and pulse signals. In the module GNSS Positioning Solution, the EKF algorithm is employed to process interfered GNSS signals for estimating the train’s position. The module Evaluation compares the calculated position with the reference position and outputs the positioning error. Fig. 2 clearly illustrates the hierarchical relationships between modules of the CPN model, providing a structured view of their layered organization. The declarations of the model are categorized and summarized in two tables for clarity. Table 1 provides the colset declarations and their respective descriptions, while Table 2 provides the variable declara- tions and their respective descriptions. 3.1. Top-level of the positioning system model The top-level of the CPN model is

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets Shuting Chena,b, Daohua Wua,b,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b National Research Center of Railway Safety Assessment, Collaborative Innovation Center of Railway Traffic Safety, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Jiang Liua,c,d, Siqi Wanga,d ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS-based train positioning Positioning performance Environment scenario GNSS signal interference Colored petri Nets Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-based continuous and accurate train positioning is one of the key technologies for advanced train operations such as train virtual coupling. However, GNSS-based train posi- tioning faces significant challenges in real-world scenarios due to environmental complexities and signal in- terferences. Considering this issue, this paper presents an approach for modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning systems using Colored Petri Nets (CPNs). By systematically modeling the GNSS signal reception and processing process, the performance of the positioning system under various environment scenarios is evaluated. The system model integrates three types of interference signals (i.e., Amplitude Modulation (AM) signals, Frequency Modulation (FM) signals, and pulse signals) while incorporating environ- mental factors such as terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding. Additionally, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) algorithm is employed to process GNSS observation data, providing accurate train position estimations. The simulation results demonstrate that signal interferences and complex environmental conditions significantly affect the GNSS-based positioning accuracy. This study offers a comprehensive framework for evaluating the performance of GNSS-based train positioning systems in different scenarios, highlighting critical factors that influence positioning accuracy and stability. 1. Introduction With the increasing demand for operation safety and efficiency in rail transportation, train virtual coupling has been proposed and be- come a hot research topic in recent years. For virtual coupling

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. J. 2025, 16, 20 7 of 34 Although LiDAR is superior to other sensors in ranging accuracy and 3D perception, its performance is poor in harsh environments such as fog, snow, or rain. Heterogeneous sensor data fusion can eliminate information redundancy and loss, and provide reliable, stable, and efficient environmental perception capabilities, but the cost of the system will increase accordingly. 2.4. Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)/Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) are key components in autonomous driving systems, helping to determine the position, speed, and orientation of the vehicle. GNSS is a satellite-based navigation system that provides precise positioning and timing information anywhere on Earth. By triangulat- ing signals from multiple satellites, a GNSS receiver can calculate the vehicle’s position (latitude, longitude, and altitude) and speed. GNSS is widely used for global positioning and mapping, especially in open areas such as highways. In autonomous driving, it is one of the most commonly used vehicle positioning sensors. GNSS provides precise global positioning, which enables accurate positioning in open environments such as highways or rural roads. Differential GPS (DGPS), Real-Time Kinematic GPS (RTK-GPS), and Precise Point Positioning (PPP) technologies can be used to improve the accuracy of GNSS. The differential global positioning system (DGPS) operation consists of a reference station and a rover. Both stations use GPS receivers to receive positioning data from GPS satellites, use the positioning data collected by the reference station to calculate the positioning error, and transmit the error correction to the rover to improve the positioning accuracy [27]. RTK can achieve real-time centimeter-level positioning accuracy through double-difference ambigu- ity resolution (AR) [28]. Compared with differential techniques, precise point positioning (PPP) has a significant advantage in that it can accurately determine the position of a GNSS rover receiver by using external corrections from the Internet or dedicated correction satellites [29]. While both DGPS and RTK can achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The system tightly couples the raw observation data of LiDAR, vision and IMU, while adding the positioning information of GNSS as a loosely coupled component to reduce the cumulative error. They use factor graph optimization methods to integrate data from different sensors and improve the accuracy and reliability of positioning through nonlinear optimization. In order to solve the limitations of SLAM algorithms in map drift, the literature [158] proposed a sensor fusion SLAM and positioning method based on LiDAR for offline mapping and online positioning of autonomous driving vehicles, integrating LiDAR with other sensors such as GNSS, IMU and vehicle status data to improve map accuracy and positioning performance, even when satellite signals are unavailable or unreliable. This type of fusion will have many advantages by combining the power of accurate LiDAR depth estimation and camera tracking capabilities. However, the fusion of LIDAR and visual SLAM will produce large cumulative errors in high- speed motion. Therefore, low-cost and high-performance inertial sensing units have become the first choice to make up for this defect [159]. Reference [160] proposed the first LiDAR–inertial–vision fusion SLAM system, LVI-SLAM. The system uses a tightly coupled framework to fuse the measurements of all three sensors, addressing the challenges of noisy point clouds, fast sensor movement, and tunnel environments. Reference [161] proposed mVIL-Fusion, a new SLAM system designed to fuse data from a monocular camera, an IMU (inertial measurement unit), and a 3D rotating LiDAR. The system aims to overcome the limitations of traditional SLAM methods, such as time synchronization issues and poor mapping performance in highly variable environments. Reference [162] proposed an effective positioning algorithm for autonomous vehicles based on vision–LiDAR–IMU fusion. In order to solve the problem of excessive back-end optimization calculations, a balanced selection strategy is adopted to reduce the computational complexity, focusing on key frames and sliding windows to optimize pose estimation. In dealing with large- scale drift, a loop detection algorithm based on iterative closest point (ICP) is proposed to improve long-term positioning accuracy. content.title: Yang - A Review of Environmental Perception Technology Based on Multi-Sensor Information Fusion in Autonomous Driving
content.abstract_or_snippet: achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The IMU can provide information about the vehicle’s attitude, velocity, and direction. The IMU helps determine vehicle motion between GNSS updates and ensures continuous tracking of the vehicle’s path, especially in areas where GNSS signals may be temporarily lost, such as tunnels and dense urban areas. The IMU provides real-time feedback on the ve- hicle’s motion, which is critical for continued tracking, especially in dynamic environments. As the positioning error accumulates during the vehicle’s driving, the IMU signal error accumulation will drift over time, so the IMU needs to continuously correct the estimated position. The data fusion of GNSS and IMU can achieve vehicle state estimation and ensure a continuous positioning process. GNSS and IMU data are often fused with other sensors such as LiDAR and radar to improve positioning accuracy and build a complete picture of the vehicle’s surroundings. By fusing these data sources, autonomous vehicles are able to navigate in a variety of environments, even without direct GNSS signals. There are currently several models for combining GNSS and IMU data. (1) GNSS/IMU integration: combining GNSS data with IMU information can create a dead reckoning system that continuously estimates the vehicle’s position when GNSS signals are unavailable. In this model, the IMU uses accelerometers and gyroscopes to track vehicle motion and orientation to fill in the gaps in GNSS data. (2) Tightly coupled system: in this, GNSS and IMU data are fused and processed in real time. This can improve the accuracy of vehicle positioning, es- pecially in challenging environments where GNSS signals are weak or unstable. (3) Loosely coupled system: in this system, GNSS and IMU operate independently and merge the data in the post-processing stage. Compared with the tightly coupled system, this method is less computationally intensive, but less accurate. World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 8 of 34 To mitigate the limitations of GNSS and IMU, these sensors are often fused with other perception technologies, such as LiDAR, radar, and cameras. GNSS/IMU systems are often integrated with LiDAR and radar sensors to enhance the vehicle’s perception of its surroundings. LiDAR provides a high-precision 3D map of the environment around the vehicle, while radar provides reliable object detection in adverse weather

content.title: Yang - A Review of Environmental Perception Technology Based on Multi-Sensor Information Fusion in Autonomous Driving
content.abstract_or_snippet: positioning system should be sufficiently robust and accurate to cope with various complex environments and severe weather conditions. Generally, in autonomous driving systems, it is a common practice to increase the overall positioning and mapping performance by fusing two or more sensors. Reference [152] proposed a camera–radar sensor fusion framework based on vehicle component (rear corner) detection and positioning to improve the stability of vehicle positioning. The main idea of this method is to enhance the azimuth accuracy of radar information by detecting and locating the rear corner of the target vehicle from the image. The proposed method can effectively track the corner points of the vehicle using only one visible corner even in occluded scenes. GPS may experience signal attenuation in indoor spaces and urban canyons, and IMU may drift over time due to error accumulation when integrating acceleration to determine velocity and position. Reference [153] uses an unscented Kalman filter (UKF) Bayesian filter to fuse GPS and IMU data. Reference [154] uses the error state Kalman filter (ESKF) and the piecewise Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS) smoothing algorithm to fuse GNSS and IMU to improve the positioning accuracy and robustness of GNSS and IMU sensors. Related works Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap- plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSS- based positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. [9] investigates the impact of continuous wave interference on GNSS receiver tracking perfor- mance, examining the extent of its influence by varying data bit durations or integration times. To address interference-related challenges, an inter- ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech- nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. The main problem and challenge for the test can be illustrated as follows. 1) Train movement simulation: the train movement needs to be si- mulated as identical to the train operation, since when the train operates the GNSS receiver mounted on the train should receive the GNSS signal, and the train moving along the track needs to have the right occupation which needs to be demonstrated on the railway simulation platform. Thus, the train maneuver dynamics should be modeled in accordance with the correct train type. An accurate train dynamics model is fundamental to train movement algorithms. 3.1. GNSS testing standard and regulations As illustrated in the introduction part, the public service performance evaluation of GNSS covers two key aspects: SIS performance and system service performance. As the BeiDou Navigation Satellite System Open Service Performance Standard (BDS-OS-PS-3.0) defines, the BDS service performance should cover 5 core indicators: positioning accuracy, velocity measurement accuracy, timing accuracy, PDOP availability, and positioning service availability, as shown in Fig. 4. In terms of positioning accuracy, the specification clarifies the statistical standard of geographical location mea- surement error and specifies the allowable error range under different geographical areas and environmental conditions. The evaluation of speed measurement accuracy is concerned with the accuracy of the user equip- ment to measure the motion speed, which is used in dynamic scenarios, such as transportation and railway applications. Timing accuracy is aimed at high-precision time synchronization services, which are critical cap- abilities in fields such as communications, energy, and scientific experi- ments. The availability of PDOP is an important index to evaluate the effect of geometric configuration on positioning accuracy and to measure the degree of support of satellite layout to the stability of positioning solution. Location service availability measures the ability to provide continuous and reliable service at a given time and area from the perspective of the overall functionality of the system. In addition, the standard also specifies the measurement methods of this performance, including the evaluation environment, data collection method, calculation method, and evaluation cycle specific requirements. content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train- centric railway signaling Baigen Caia,b,⁎ a School of Computer and Information Technology, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c School of Geodesy and Geomatics, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China d GNSS Research Center, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China e Space Engineering University, Beijing 101416, China , Jingnan Liuc,d, Xurong Donge, Jiang Liub ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: Railway signaling Train control Global Navigation Satellite System Sensor data fusion Virtual Balise Train integrity Performance evaluation The application of Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) in the intelligent railway systems is rapidly developing all over the world. With the GNSS-based train positioning and moving state perception, the au- tonomy and flexibility of a novel train control system can be greatly enhanced over the existing solutions relying on the track-side facilities. Considering the safety critical features of the railway signaling applications, the GNSS stand-alone mode may not be sufficient to satisfy the practical requirements. In this paper, the key technologies for applying GNSS in novel train-centric railway signaling systems are investigated, including the multi-sensor data fusion, Virtual Balise (VB) capturing and messaging, train integrity monitoring and system performance evaluation. According to the practical characteristics of the novel train control system under the moving block mode, the details of the key technologies are introduced. Field demonstration results of a novel train control system using the presented technologies under the practical railway operation conditions are presented to il- lustrate the achievable performance feature of autonomous train state perception using BeiDou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) and related solutions. It reveals the great potentials of these key technologies in the next generation train control system and other GNSS-based railway implementations. 1. Introduction The train control system plays a significant role in ensuring the safety of railway operation and improving the transportation efficiency. Chinese Train Control Systems (CTCSs), including CTCS level 2 and level 3, have been

However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. Virtual Balise The concept of VB (Virtual Balise) can effectively integrate the sa- tellite positioning technology into existing train control system frame- works without modification to the specifications [7,8]. VB capture is acknowledged by matching the train position and the preset VB re- ference position along the track [9]. Within a specific distance limit, a success matching will enable the determination of the time instant when a train passes through the target VB. Then, the VB telegram can be ex- tracted and transmitted to the ATP (Automatic Train Protection) system kernel. Based on the existing VB capture mechanism, a pre-capture de- cision logic is necessary to effectively reduce the risk of missed VB capturing. A VB capture recognition method based on the forward search strategy is proposed to accurately determine the position and time of VB capture, which improves the VB capture performance over conventional solution requires a pre-defined capture interval. 3.1. Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises.
